reasoning:
Confirmed. Two distinct wp_remote_get call sites have their destination URL controlled by apply_filters() with no URL validation guard of any kind.

1. fetch_and_cache_mappings() (line ~73): $json_url = ProxyUtils::get_plugin_mappings_api_url() -> Filters::apply_plugin_mappings_api_url() -> apply_filters('leadin_plugin_mappings_api_url', "$base_url/cos-domains/v1/wp-plugin-mappings/get-all"). This return value is passed verbatim to wp_remote_get($json_url, ...). No wp_http_validate_url(), no allowlist, no esc_url_raw guard.

2. proxy_requests() (line ~199): $target_url = ProxyUtils::get_proxy_base_url() . $proxy_path -> Filters::apply_sites_proxy_cdn_filters() -> apply_filters('leadin_sites_proxy_cdn', "https://$portal_id.$domain.net"). Concatenated with $proxy_path and passed verbatim to wp_remote_get($target_url, ...). Same absence of validation.

A hooked plugin returning 'http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/' (AWS metadata) or any internal service URL from either filter achieves SSRF. In the proxy_requests() case, the $proxy_path is derived from cached mappings or predefined patterns, but the base URL is fully attacker-controlled.

Reachability: Requires code execution via another installed plugin or theme — add_filter() has no privilege check, it fires at plugin/theme load time. This is not direct web-user reachability. The proxy feature must be enabled (get_proxy_mappings_enabled() = true), which is the case when the HubSpot proxy is configured. Severity medium is correct: real SSRF capability but gated behind plugin-level code execution (not unauthenticated web request).
